_note: Phase 0 measurement gate for the indexing perf overhaul (specs A/B/C). Captured on an idle machine. Recall baseline still pending (needs the 20-query golden set = first implementation task).

gpu.enablement_recipe: pip install onnxruntime-gpu==1.26.0 + nvidia-cudnn-cu12 + nvidia-cublas-cu12 + nvidia-cuda-runtime-cu12 (+cufft,curand,nvrtc); then ort.preload_dlls(); then TextEmbedding(providers=['CUDAExecutionProvider','CPUExecutionProvider']) or fastembed cuda=True AFTER preload_dlls
gpu.gotcha: cuda=True and explicit CUDA providers BOTH silently fall back to CPU until (a) the nvidia CUDA/cuDNN pip packages are installed AND (b) ort.preload_dlls() is called. Always verify model.model.model.get_providers() to detect silent CPU fallback.

rss_14gb_root_cause.confirmed: onnxruntime CPU activation arena grows with batch_size x sequence_length and is not released (batch 64 -> 4.1GB; the real _BATCH_SIZE=400 -> ~14GB)
rss_14gb_root_cause.debunked_hypothesis: graph_chunks: list[Chunk] accumulation is only 135MB - NOT the cause
rss_14gb_root_cause.implication: On GPU the arena lives in VRAM (12GB), so host RSS stays low; the chunk-size cap + token-budget batching remain valuable for CPU-fallback memory safety and recall quality, not as the primary perf lever

threads.meaning: 0 = auto = all physical cores; explicit thread-tuning is likely YAGNI

file_walk.conclusion: rglob is NOT a perf bottleneck (<0.4s); switching to git ls-files is a SECURITY fix (exclude gitignored), not a performance one

chunks_axon.conclusion: giant-chunk tail is real (max 52KB); chunk-size cap justified for VRAM/CPU memory + recall bounding; 0 chunk-id collisions so the D1 stable-id migration is safe

qdrant_points.conclusion: all current data sits in 'personal' (old uncapped chunking); 'knowledge' (the new target ctx) is empty -> clean purge+reindex migration

priority_reordering: GPU embedding (Spec B) is the dominant lever - PROVEN 541/s, exceeds the >=300/s target, and removes the host-RSS problem. Smallest effort, biggest win.
Chunk-size cap + token-budget batching (Spec A) drop from #1 perf lever to memory-safety + CPU-fallback + recall-quality bounding.
Incremental cache (Spec C) remains the refresh-cost lever (full GPU reindex is ~9s for all 9 repos, so even a full re-embed is cheap on GPU - lowers the urgency of the cache somewhat).
Per-machine dependency: desktop = onnxruntime-gpu + CUDA libs; M1 Pro mac = onnxruntime + CoreML EP. Must be conditional (do NOT pin onnxruntime-gpu in shared pyproject).
still_pending: GPU peak VRAM measurement under batch 400 + big chunks (confirm 12GB headroom).
M1 Pro CoreML throughput (run on the mac).
Recall baseline (20-query golden set) - first implementation task; chunking changes must not regress it.
